hook: You already know how to split a pizza or measure half a cup of flour — you've been using fractions your whole life. But when it comes to adding, comparing, or dividing them, things can get tricky without a solid system. This unit gives you that system, so fractions go from confusing to completely under your control.
concept: Every topic in this unit builds on one central idea: a fraction is just a way of expressing a part of a whole, and once you understand that relationship, you can manipulate fractions in any way you need — formally known as operating on rational numbers.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn what the numerator and denominator represent and how to find equivalent fractions like $\frac{1}{2} = \frac{3}{6}$ by multiplying or dividing both parts by the same number.
Master reducing fractions to their simplest form by dividing the numerator and denominator by their greatest common factor, so $\frac{8}{12}$ becomes $\frac{2}{3}$.
You will learn to compare fractions like $\frac{3}{4}$ and $\frac{5}{8}$ by finding a common denominator and correctly placing them in order from least to greatest.
Apply the skill of finding common denominators to accurately add and subtract fractions, including those with different denominators like $\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{4}$.
You will learn to multiply fractions efficiently by multiplying numerators together and denominators together, and simplify the result to lowest terms.
Master dividing fractions by applying the "keep, change, flip" method — multiplying by the reciprocal — to solve problems like $\frac{3}{4} \div \frac{1}{2}$.
You will learn to convert fluently between mixed numbers like $2\frac{1}{3}$ and improper fractions like $\frac{7}{3}$, a skill that unlocks the next level of fraction work.
Apply everything you've learned to add, subtract, multiply, and divide mixed numbers, solving real-world problems that involve quantities greater than one whole.
